segments.text: We have our last candidate now, Rebecca Greenspan.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: She's on special leave from her role as Secretary-General of UNCTAD, the UN Trade and Development.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: She's the first woman to head UNCTAD in its 60-year history.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Before that, she served as Under-Secretary-General of the United Nations Development Program, UNDP, Secretary-General of the Ibero-American Conference,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: and Vice President of Costa Rica, and she is nominated by her country, Costa Rica.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Please join me in welcoming Rebecca Greenspan.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: It's so good to see you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Nice to see you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Last but not least,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: You have five minutes to convince us.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: What is your vision as UN Secretary General?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Why would you want the job and how will you do it?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And at four minutes, I'll give you a one minute warning.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Can I do this so I don't give you my back?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I can see all of you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Is that okay with you?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Yes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Okay.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So why am I running?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Yes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I have said that the UN
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And the world today is in a difficult moment.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Conflicts are going up, as everybody has said, and they are on the peak since 1945.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Trust in the organization is going down, and time is running out.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And I believe in the UN Charter.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I believe in the principles of the UN Charter.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I have said,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: the daughter of peace, of two refugees of the Second World War that were able to have a dignified life because they found peace and respect in Costa Rica.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And I know what war takes away from you and what peace makes possible.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And that's why I'm running, because this is a consequential moment
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: for the international system, for international law, for the UN, and I want to be part of the solution.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I want to be the leader of an organization that means so much for so many.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Now, I am defending the UN, but not necessarily I am defending the UN as it is.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: The UN has to reform and has to be more agile, more flexible, more nimble in certain ways.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: It has to learn how to partner with others.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: You know, in the UN, we have this mentality that everything has to happen inside the UN.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: not only countries have more capacities, but there are regional organizations today that did not exist in 1945.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And we need to allow new actors to take a center stage in challenges that the world is facing.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: The private sector has more capacities today, civil society.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So it's not only to bring them to the UN, it's for the UN to go to them and be able to support what they do, the things that they can solve, also help us to solve them.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And we are too accustomed to compete all the time between us and with the external world to be alone in the centerpiece of everything.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And when I say that the UN has to change and I have used a phrase that has been misinterpreted because I have said the UN cannot do more with less because we cannot do that forever.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So we need to do less with less.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: But let me correct that and say we have to be more strategic in what we do.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: We cannot just add on with less resources all the time.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: My second point is peace.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And there we have to, you know, I have going around when I step down from the ANTA job to dedicate myself to the campaign.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And people ask me the same question.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Where is the UN?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: You know, in these scenarios of conflict around the world, they don't see us.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And it's true that preventive diplomacy is something that you don't see.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: But it's also true that there are things happening
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: in the negotiations, where conflicts are being resolved, where agreements are being forged, and the UN is not there.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So my second point is that the UN has to be back at the table.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And for that, the office of the Secretary General has to change.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: The office has to be structured to have the capacity to support the good offices of the Secretary General.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: That is the one thing that is clear in the Charter as the task of the Secretary General.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And we have to reform ourselves to do that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And I can expand on that if you want.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: You have 30 seconds.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And third, we need to think about the future.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: We have to cooperate for the future.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Young people are anxious about their jobs.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Women are anxious about their rights.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So let's have also positive agenda.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: There are so many good things happening at the same time.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Let's be able to have, you know, a coalition for cooperation for the future that makes sense for everybody.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: The toughest of times, some would say the toughest of times in the UN's 80 years.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: What gives Rebecca Greenspan the skills, the experience to make sure the UN is there?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: What do you have that others haven't had?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Well, first of all, I have done a lot of things in my life.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: But this is a difficult challenge.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: What have you done that makes you qualify for this job?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: I know that for many people this is an impossible job.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: It has been described that way.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Yes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I have had to deal with very difficult situations.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: When I started my career in Costa Rica, we were in the last decade of Latin America.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: We had defaulted in our debt, and I was in the Ministry of Finance.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And I had to navigate that situation that was with huge constraints, with huge pressures on you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And I have learned to live under pressure, to take decisions, to take tough decisions, and also to be able to have the team and be the team leader that is necessary for those situations.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So I believe I am experienced, I am confident, I am determined, and I am a good leader.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So I can be the secretary and the general at the same time.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: More general than secretary?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: That's what the survey of the Global South said, that's what they want, more general than secretary.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Yeah, but you know, there is part of being a secretary that will be very important also this time.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Yes?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: That is your management skills.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: If you are going to reform the organization, you have to drive management and not allow management to drive you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: But you might have to be a general, maybe even a field marshal to put the UN at the centre.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Let's get some commitments from you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: This is going to be on the record.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: People are listening and taking notes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: The One for Eight Billion campaign and the UN Association of the UK as a founding partner has called on all candidates during this campaign to commit
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: publicly to five principles.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: You may know them.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: One, disclosing financial, publishing financial disclosures, engaging with civil society, participating in transparent
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: General Assembly and Security Council processes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Remember, no one wants it behind closed doors.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Respecting Article 100 of the UN Charter, in other words, independence from member states.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Nobody wants one or two or three countries to dominate.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And avoiding conflicts of interest with any current UN roles.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Do you accept these principles?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Absolutely.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Yeah, I think that they make sense.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: If you want a secretary general that is able to bring back trust in the organization, it has to start with integrity and with accountability.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And I am absolutely willing to do that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: We're going to hold you to it.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Let's go to some of the...
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: I hope so.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Me and everybody.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Yes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Everyone here tonight and many more.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: First question from the audience, Anne Kaproff from the UN.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: She's in the United States.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: The UN was founded to maintain peace and security, yet critics argue it's, well, as you said, increasingly absent from critical peace efforts.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: How would you restore the organization's relevance, good offices, and trusted role in conflict diplomacy?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Now, we've touched on that, but what we've heard from previous secretary generals when you say, well, the Security Council is gridlock, what can we do?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We only are as good as our member states.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: What would you do differently?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Do you want to reform it?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Do you want to take it?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: How do you want to approach this?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Well, I said and I repeat that the office of the Secretary General has to be restructured.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: You cannot do mediation and prevention in a
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: pyramidal, bureaucratic structure.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Bureaucracies are very important.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I come from Costa Rica.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I supported the civil service of my country, yes?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: But you cannot ask from a bureaucracy to deliver on things they are not.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: about to do.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And to have flexibility, to be agile, is not part of a bureaucratic structure that is, at the same time, pyramidal.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And that's why, you know, when we were negotiating the Black Sea Initiative, five people worked with me.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: This was in the Ukraine War.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: That's for the grain.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Exactly.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: When we opened up the Black Sea for Trade, that was so important to avoid a food insecurity crisis in the world.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And, you know, I had a very small team, five people that reported only to me, and I reported to the Secretary General.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And it worked.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Yes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: You know, everything that we wanted to do after that was stopped.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So you need a structure that will be flexible, that will be accountable to the Secretary General, and you need the best team ever.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: You know, the Secretary General has to have really robust
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: and qualified people that are able to take this agenda forward.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Good.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: A question from the audience now.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Aziel Goulandres, Secretary General of the World Federation of UN Associations from Switzerland.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Good, you're here.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Thank you for coming all the way from Switzerland.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: What's the name of this?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: The City of Peace.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Yes, your question.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: My name is Aziel Goulandris.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I am with the World Federation of UN Associations.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Thank you for being here.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Thank you to UNAUK for organizing this.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: The United Nations often, very often, consults civil society.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: But consultation does not always mean that it translates into influence.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: So if elected Secretary General, what mechanism
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: could we establish, could be established to ensure meaningful and geographically balanced civil society participation, including UN associations like this one, to ensure that their input affects decisions, not only discussions.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Thank you very much.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: A mechanism.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Yeah, I know that in the past there was a representation in the Office of the Secretary General of Civil Society that had
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: that role and that's something to be thought about and restored probably for that to happen.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: But also let me say we need to hear civil society and partner with civil society not only because of money, it's not because of financial resources, it's also knowledge and capacities.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And that's why I meant that we need to partner much more.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: We need to get a mechanism.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: You're already talking about less with less, so you don't want to create new, but.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Less is refocusing, not downsizing.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Would you establish?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I already answered that there was a mechanism in the Office of the Secretary General that could be restored.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: OK. That's what I said.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: But I think that civil society also
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I'm sorry to say, should not only think about how they influence the UN inside, but how they bring the UN outside.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: They can help with that, not only on the one-sided way in which it has always been thought of.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Another question from the floor, I understand.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Umar Diallo, Association des Familles des Martres de Senegal.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Vous êtes ici?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Omar?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Oh, oh.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Senegal?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We moved yellow.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Hello, I'm Omou Diallo and I'm here representing Collective des Familles des Martyrs du Senegal entre 2021 et 2024.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: My question is, how would you strengthen accountability and protection for victims of political repression in countries where state institutions are perceived as unwilling or unable to investigate abuses independently?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: How would you strengthen accountability and protection for victims of political repression in countries where state institutions are
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: perceived as unwilling or unable to investigate abuses independently?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Well, the main mechanism that we have is the human rights pillar and they have mechanisms to do that and it has to be put forward that that is happening and to ask for the intervention of the human rights commissioner.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And I think that in the protection when civil rights have been abused, the main mechanism that the UN has is the human rights pillar.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: and maybe thinking aloud now that we have the question, we haven't explained that around.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: People don't know what are the mechanisms in the UN that we could use.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And that takes my attention to one problem that I haven't thought about, that maybe many people don't know.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: What are the avenues or mechanisms that they can use to protect themselves with the UN?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And so that's probably a problem of communication because we speak in a way that nobody understands.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: But also a problem of educating the population by the presence of the UN on the ground for them to know what are the mechanisms that can be enacted for that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Thank you very much for your question and it's an interesting reflection by you because certainly the survey which was done about the Global South, great dissatisfaction, disappointment with what the UN has done.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: done or not done.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Most of all, a vast majority is saying it's too dominated by the powerful state.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So people are asking now for mechanisms which you say exist, but they're obviously not working well, not doing their job.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Another question... Well, they do their job in many parts.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Well, let's see, but there seems to be tonight an idea they've got to do a lot better.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Is that right?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Yeah.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Ishan Shah from the UN Foundation is here.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Ishan?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Oh, thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Thank you so much.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Thank you, Ms. Greenspan, for being here today as well and for Dialogue Missile Society.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Through the World's Toughest Job Campaign by UN Foundation, thousands of young people helped write a job description for the next Secretary-General, which I have here.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: They are asking to work with leaders to reform how the UN delivers for their communities as opposed to carry its consequences when expectations fall short.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So in your first year, what would you actually change in the Executive Office of the Secretary General, in the UN system, and the way priorities are set to ensure young people have real influence from the beginning, rather than being invited in only after priorities, budgets, agendas, and reforms have already been decided?

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Thank you.

segments.text: But probably that mechanism needs strengthening.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And I have said in my vision statement that we will.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: That one of the things that we are worried about is about the arms of the young people.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: in front of a world that is changing a job space that they don't know how it will be, et cetera.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: I have to say we've received so many questions from young people saying they just don't feel there's a way to get them into the system and give them a real voice.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Another question, Jordan Ryan, former Assistant Secretary General of the UNDP.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Maybe you know him.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: The UN faces an acute crisis of funding, legitimacy and political confidence.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: What would you do in your first year to protect the independence of the Secretary General?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: reduce the organization's vulnerability to political blackmail by major contributors, no names mentioned, and prove that the UN can still act in the common interest.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: You've talked about your office.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: What would you do in your first year to solidify that independence against powerful voices?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Well, we have to fix the financial problem of the UN, yes?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And there are three things there.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: First, we have to ask and demand as UN a stable panorama of finance.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: No organization can reform and do all what we have to do if there is total uncertainty about the budget and the availability of resources in the organization.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Second, you will have to convince those that have the responsibility to finance the UN with assessed contributions, not only voluntary contributions, but assessed contributions that are obligations of the state.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Now, if you look at the panorama today, the US and China will represent 44% of the financing of the UN.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: We have to ask ourselves, is that sustainable?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Actually, it's 44% because we put a cap on the maximum.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: If not, the US will be paying 25% right now, and probably China will go in two years to be also 25%.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I think that this is a very difficult discussion.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And let me tell you that most of these experiments have avoided it.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Yeah, it goes to the heart of the problem now.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: I think so.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And I think that we have to put that on the table and find the collision of countries that are willing to open up at least that discussion.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: But there is a third problem that we have, and it's the financial rules.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Because the financial rules that are right now imposed on the UN, they ask us to, and I don't want to enter into all the technicalities of the mechanism.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: You've got 30 seconds.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Yes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: This is... You won't solve it tonight, but just one last...
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I am not saying I'm solving it tonight, but this is a difficult thing to understand.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: We have to give back money that we haven't received according to the financial rules that there are today.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And so what I am telling the countries is that they have to give us a moratorium at least of some of the financial rules for us to be able
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: to breathe in the next two to three years and solve the problem because we have to create the trust and the political will in the UN again also to solve the financial problem.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So we need these three things to happen.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: It will certainly be the toughest of jobs, time consuming.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Please give us a reassurance before you leave this stage.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: You'll find a way to relax so you don't collapse on the job.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: I never collapse.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And do you relax?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: I do relax with my family.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Well, tell us one thing.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: My family, I love to be at home.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: You know, I always on the run.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I am always in airports and airplanes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: When I go home, I just want to be home and talk to my granddaughter every day.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: That helps a lot.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Rebecca Greenstein, thank you for sharing time with us.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Thank you very much, Rebecca.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Really good to see you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01
